faq.q: What if I get rejected when I make the first move?
faq.a: A rejection is a moment — quickly forgotten by both sides. It says nothing about your worth. And each move you dare to make, even an unsuccessful one, makes the next one easier.

title: Daring to Be Vulnerable
intro: Sharing your doubts and weaknesses with the right people builds real intimacy. It's a strength, not a flaw.

sections.heading: Vulnerability creates closeness
sections.body: Relationships stay surface-level as long as everyone is shielded behind a perfect facade. Real closeness only happens when someone dares to show a doubt, a fear, a struggle. Shared vulnerability invites the other person to open up too. A deep friendship is recognizable precisely by this: the possibility of not pretending to be fine.

sections.heading: Choose who you open up to
sections.body: Opening up doesn't mean telling everything to everyone. Vulnerability is shared with people who've proven themselves trustworthy and respectful. With them, dare. With people you barely know or who've already broken your trust, tread carefully. That discernment protects vulnerability without suffocating it.

sections.heading: Vulnerability isn't weakness
sections.body: Our culture sometimes ties fragility to weakness — especially for men. That's a mistake. Admitting that you're scared, that you doubt, that you need help takes courage. Constantly hiding everything is exhausting and isolating. People who can be openly vulnerable often inspire more trust and respect than those who perform invincibility.

steps: Accept that real relationships require some vulnerability.
Choose trustworthy people to open up to.
Dare to share a doubt or a struggle, not just your wins.
Remember that acknowledging a weakness is an act of courage.

faq.q: What if someone uses my vulnerability against me?
faq.a: That's the risk with the wrong person — which is why choosing who you open up to matters. With a reliable friend, vulnerability strengthens the bond; it doesn't weaken you.

title: Calibrating Your Expectations of Others
intro: Many relational disappointments come from unrealistic or unstated expectations. Recalibrating them eases your relationships.

sections.heading: The unspoken expectation
sections.body: We often expect things from others we've never actually voiced: that they'll guess our needs, think of us, respond the way we would. Then we're disappointed they didn't read our minds. No one can do that. An unexpressed expectation is a disappointment waiting to happen. The first step is putting clear words to what you actually expect.

sections.heading: Expectations tailored to each person
sections.body: Every person has their strengths and their limits. Expecting a party-loving friend to be your crisis confidant, or expecting big declarations from someone naturally reserved, leads to frustration. Learn to see people as they are and expect from each one what they can genuinely give. You can appreciate someone without expecting everything from them.

sections.heading: Say it instead of stewing
sections.body: When an expectation really matters to you, express it instead of ruminating on the disappointment. 'I would have liked you to call that day — it matters to me.' The other person can then adjust, or explain their constraints. A lot of quiet resentments would dissolve with one honest sentence. Clear, spoken expectations damage relationships far less than silent disappointment.

steps: Identify the expectations you've never clearly voiced.
Adjust what you expect to each person's actual strengths.
Express your important expectations rather than keeping them in.
Stop expecting one single person to meet all your needs.

faq.q: Isn't lowering my expectations just giving up?
faq.a: No. It's making them realistic and expressing them — not abandoning your needs. A fair, spoken expectation has a much better chance of being met.

title: Shining in High-Stakes Social Situations
intro: Interviews, presentations, oral exams — these moments feel like being put on trial. The right approach makes them far more manageable.

sections.heading: Reframe the situation
sections.body: We experience these moments as a courtroom where conviction looms. Reframe them: a job interview is also an exchange where you're evaluating whether the role is right for you; a presentation is a chance to share what you've learned. You're not just being judged — you're an active participant. That shift cuts the feeling of being passive prey.

sections.heading: Preparation as a stress antidote
sections.body: A large part of the stress comes from the unknown. Prepare: research, anticipate likely questions, practice your key answers out loud, prepare your own questions. The more you know the material, the less you fear blanking. Preparation doesn't eliminate nerves, but it gives you a solid foundation to stand on when emotion rises.

sections.heading: Managing the moment itself
sections.body: On the day, breathe slowly before you start, speak at a steady pace, and allow yourself pauses to think. If a question catches you off guard, you can say 'Give me a moment.' That's not a weakness — it's thoughtfulness. And remember that the person across from you isn't your enemy: most of the time, they want things to go well for you too.

steps: Reframe the situation: you're a participant, not just a subject.
Prepare likely questions and practice your answers out loud.
Breathe slowly just before you begin.
Allow yourself pauses to think — without guilt.

faq.q: How do I hide my stress in an interview?
faq.a: A bit of nerves is normal — and usually invisible. Speak slowly, ground your feet, breathe. Interviewers expect nerves and don't hold them against you.

title: Letting a Relationship Go
intro: Not every relationship is meant to last forever. Knowing how to let go — peacefully — is part of a healthy social life.

sections.heading: Some relationships have a natural end
sections.body: School friendships, bonds tied to a place or a period of life — many connections are made for a chapter, not for all of it. When a bond quietly fades because paths have diverged, it's not a failure or anyone's fault. It's the normal movement of life. Accepting that saves you from unnecessary guilt.

sections.heading: Let go without resentment
sections.body: Letting a relationship go doesn't mean turning it into a bad memory. You can look back with gratitude for what it brought you while recognizing it belongs to the past. That kind of peace is different from resentment: it closes the chapter gently, without bitterness, and leaves you free and light for what's ahead.

sections.heading: Make room for the present
sections.body: Clinging to a finished relationship out of fear of the void blocks you from seeing the connections that are present and coming. When you let go of what's over, you free up time, energy, and attention for the people who are genuinely there today. A living social life renews itself — it welcomes the new as much as it honors the old.

steps: Accept that some relationships belong to a chapter of your life.
Distinguish a natural ending from a failure or a fault.
Hold gratitude rather than resentment.
Invest your energy in present and future connections.

faq.q: How do I know whether to fight for a relationship or let it go?
faq.a: Make one genuine, clear effort first. If the bond doesn't respond — or mostly brings you pain — letting go becomes healthy. You'll fight for the ones who show up.

title: Balancing What You Give and What You Receive
intro: Lasting relationships rest on reciprocity. Giving too much or taking too much always tips the balance — and eventually breaks it.

sections.heading: The trap of giving too much
sections.body: Some people give constantly: they listen, help, make themselves available, and never dare ask for anything in return. That imbalance seems generous, but it's exhausting — and over time breeds quiet resentment. It also teaches the other person to receive without giving back. Giving too much isn't healthier than taking too much: a relationship needs both to flow.

sections.heading: Learning to receive
sections.body: Receiving is a relational skill in its own right. Letting someone help you, support you, do something nice for you — it gives them an active role in the relationship and shows you trust them. Consistently refusing all help, in the name of self-sufficiency, denies them the satisfaction of being useful and cuts you off from genuine support.

sections.heading: Balance over the long haul
sections.body: Reciprocity isn't a strict ledger where everyone gives back exactly what they got. It plays out over time with flexibility: sometimes one person gives more, then it swings the other way. What matters is the overall sense of balance. If you feel a heavy, lasting imbalance, that's something to name calmly — not stew over in silence.

steps: Check whether you're giving significantly more than you receive.
Accept the help and care that people offer you.
Dare to ask when you need something.
Assess the balance over time and name a lasting imbalance.

faq.q: Isn't keeping track of what you give and receive petty?
faq.a: It's not about precise accounting — it's about sensing the overall balance. A healthy relationship feels effortless, but it doesn't hold together under permanent imbalance.
